title: Practice: zero, first and second conditionals

questions.prompt: Complete the zero conditional: "If you mix red and blue, you ___ purple."
questions.explanation: Mixing red and blue always gives purple, so this is a general truth = zero conditional, which uses the present simple in both clauses: you get purple. "will get" would make it a first conditional (a specific future result); "would get" is second conditional (imaginary); "got" is past simple and does not fit a timeless fact.

questions.prompt: Complete the first conditional: "If she ___ harder, she will pass the baccalaureate."
questions.explanation: In the first conditional, the if-clause uses the present simple and "will" appears only in the main clause: if she studies harder, she will pass. So "will study" inside the if-clause is the classic mistake; "studied"/"would study" belong to the second conditional (imaginary), not this realistic situation.

questions.prompt: Complete the second conditional: "If I ___ a superhero, I would fly to school."
questions.explanation: Being a superhero is imaginary, so this is a second conditional. In standard English the verb "be" takes "were" for all persons in the if-clause: if I were a superhero. "am" is present (real); "will be" belongs to the first conditional; "would be" is for the main clause, not the if-clause.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a comma?

options.text: I will help you, if you ask me.
If you ask me I will help you.
If, you ask me I will help you.
If you ask me, I will help you.

questions.explanation: When the if-clause comes first, place a comma after it: "If you ask me, I will help you." No comma is used when the main clause comes first.

questions.prompt: Rewrite using "unless": "If you don't eat breakfast, you will feel weak." Choose the correct version:

options.text: Unless you don't eat breakfast, you will feel weak.
Unless you ate breakfast, you would feel weak.
Unless you eat breakfast, you would feel weak.
Unless you eat breakfast, you will feel weak.

questions.explanation: "Unless" already means "if ... not", so the verb stays positive: unless you eat breakfast, you will feel weak. Using "don't" after "unless" creates a double negative.

questions.prompt: Identify the conditional type: "If animals could talk, the world would be very different."
questions.explanation: If + past simple (could talk) + would + base verb (would be) = second conditional. Animals cannot really talk — it is a hypothetical, unreal situation.
